source.submodule: Objects as parameters, Friend function & Aggregation

topicTags: Objects as parameters, Friend function & Aggregation

content.problemStatement:
You are tasked with implementing a C++ class to represent complex numbers. Complex numbers have a real part (a) and an imaginary part (b), where i represents the square root of -1.

Define a Complex class with the following attributes and methods:
- real: A double representing the real part of the complex number.
- imaginary: A double representing the imaginary part of the complex number.
- A constructor that initializes the real and imaginary parts of the complex number.
- A display method that displays the complex number in the format real + imaginaryi, where real and imaginary are the real and imaginary parts of the complex number, respectively.
- Implement a friend function named addComplex that takes two Complex objects as parameters and returns a new Complex object that represents the sum of the two input complex numbers. The friend function should be declared and defined outside the Complex class.

### Task

Create two Complex objects, complex1 and complex2, with initial values which are already provided in template.
Use the addComplex friend function to add complex1 and complex2 and store the result in a third Complex object, result.
Display the values of complex1, complex2, and result.
constraints: - The real and imaginary parts of the complex numbers are double-precision floating-point numbers.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
